claims.plain: The Navier-Stokes Millennium Problem remains officially unsolved.
claims.formal: Clay Mathematics Institute lists the Navier-Stokes Equation as Unsolved.
claims.conclusion: No generally accepted proof of Clay alternative A, B, C, or D is recognized.
claims.audit: Current-status claim; recheck before time-sensitive reporting.

claims.plain: Proving any one of four official alternatives resolves the stated problem.
claims.formal: Fefferman's statement requests a proof of A, B, C, or D.
claims.conclusion: Either a global-smoothness theorem or an admissible breakdown construction can resolve the problem.
claims.audit: Narrative paraphrases must defer to the official quantifiers.

claims.plain: Velocity L3 and vorticity L(3/2) are invariant under Navier-Stokes scaling.
claims.formal: For u_lambda=lambda u(lambda x,lambda^2 t), the Lq exponent is 1-3/q; for omega_lambda=lambda^2 omega(lambda x,lambda^2 t), it is 2-3/q.
assumptions: The whole-space scaling is applied without boundary effects.
claims.conclusion: q=3 for velocity and q=3/2 for vorticity are spatially critical.
claims.audit: Mechanically checked by lab.navier_lab.scaling.

claims.plain: The unforced smooth equation dissipates kinetic energy.
claims.formal: One half d/dt ||u||_2^2 plus nu ||grad u||_2^2 equals zero.
claims.conclusion: Kinetic energy cannot increase in the smooth unforced evolution.
claims.audit: Energy control alone is not a regularity theorem.

claims.plain: Global finite-energy weak solutions exist in three dimensions.
claims.formal: Leray's construction produces global weak solutions with energy control for finite-energy divergence-free data.
claims.conclusion: Weak global existence holds, without proving global smoothness.
claims.audit: Do not upgrade weak existence to classical existence.

claims.plain: The possible singular set of a suitable weak solution is very small in parabolic measure.
claims.formal: The one-dimensional parabolic Hausdorff measure of the singular set is zero under the CKN hypotheses.
claims.conclusion: The singular set is restricted but not proven empty.
claims.audit: Partial regularity is not global regularity.

claims.plain: Uniform-in-time spatial L3 control of velocity prevents blow-up.
claims.formal: A suitable/Leray-Hopf solution in L-infinity(0,T;L3(R3)) is regular under the theorem's hypotheses.
assumptions: Velocity bounded in L-infinity time and L3 space
claims.conclusion: The solution is regular through the terminal time.
claims.audit: This is strong L3, not weak-L3.

claims.plain: Finite-q critical Lorentz control of velocity is a regularity class.
claims.formal: L-infinity time L(3,q) space with finite q implies regularity in the stated setting.
claims.conclusion: The solution is regular.
claims.audit: The q=infinity weak endpoint is excluded from this claim.

claims.plain: Sufficient coherence of vorticity direction in intense regions depletes stretching enough for regularity.
claims.formal: The Constantin-Fefferman geometric hypothesis implies regularity under its precise integrability and coherence assumptions.
claims.conclusion: Finite-time blow-up is excluded.
claims.audit: The geometry is a hypothesis, not known to be universal.

claims.plain: An important integrable class of backward self-similar singularities is impossible.
claims.formal: The only Leray self-similar profile in the specified L3 class is trivial.
claims.conclusion: That self-similar blow-up class is excluded.
claims.audit: Does not exclude every asymptotic, discrete, or Type-II scenario.

claims.plain: A continuously backward self-similar Leray profile in weak L3 is zero.
claims.formal: Guevara-Phuc Theorem 1.3 states that a weak solution U of the Leray profile equation in W1,2 locally and L(q,infinity) is identically zero for every q strictly between 12/5 and 6; q equals 3 is included.
claims.conclusion: The profile and its continuously backward self-similar flow vanish.
claims.audit: The theorem excludes exact continuous backward self-similarity. It does not exclude discrete or asymptotic self-similarity, scale-aperiodic ancient flows, or general L-infinity time weak-L3 endpoint solutions.

claims.plain: Scaling and energy cancellation do not by themselves force regularity.
claims.formal: An averaged Navier-Stokes bilinear operator preserving key scaling and cancellation properties admits finite-time blow-up.
assumptions: Replace the true nonlinearity by Tao's averaged operator
claims.conclusion: A regularity proof must use finer structure of the true nonlinearity.
claims.audit: This is not blow-up for the true Navier-Stokes equation.

claims.plain: Finite-energy distributional Navier-Stokes solutions can be nonunique in a weak class.
claims.formal: Buckmaster and Vicol construct distinct weak solutions sharing data in their stated finite-energy class.
assumptions: The weak solution class used in the source
claims.conclusion: Uniqueness fails in that weak class.
claims.audit: Do not restate as smooth-data classical blow-up or general Leray-Hopf nonuniqueness.

claims.plain: Forced Leray solutions can be nonunique for the same force and the same zero initial velocity.
claims.formal: Albritton, Brue, and Colombo construct two distinct Leray solutions with identical force and zero initial velocity.
assumptions: The exact force and solution class in the source
claims.conclusion: Uniqueness fails in the constructed forced setting.
claims.audit: Must not be promoted to Clay C without matching Fefferman's smooth force and classical conclusion.

claims.plain: A 2026 preprint claims certified unforced Leray-Hopf nonuniqueness from singular critical initial data.
claims.formal: The source claims infinitely many suitable Leray-Hopf solutions with the same compactly supported singular initial condition and smoothness for positive times.
claims.conclusion: Unforced suitable Leray-Hopf nonuniqueness in the claimed class.
claims.audit: Independent certificate reproduction pending; singular initial data lie outside Clay A-D.

claims.plain: A 2026 preprint conditionally excludes a structured critical point singularity under log-weighted vorticity-direction control; the full proof chain has been independently reconstructed here with explicit repairs.
claims.formal: For a normalized projected mild solution, uniform weak-L(3/2) vorticity, uniform high-level fixed-centre localization, and a global unit direction extension with uniform bmo_(1/|log r|) control on one terminal interval imply that the proposed T-star is not singular.
assumptions: Fixed-centre uniform critical upper envelope or equivalent uniform high-level single-ball localization
Existence of a global unit direction extension with uniform log-weighted BMO control
Projected mild velocity normalization or a uniformly bounded spatially constant harmonic background
claims.conclusion: The proposed first singular time is regular.
claims.audit: O2607-01 through O2607-16 are closed by independent analytic reconstructions. The proof survives after documented repairs to profile and direction quantifiers, weak-Lorentz commutator interpolation, shell endpoints, the terminal interval, slowly varying inversions, velocity normalization, escape-time selection, slit-domain harmonic measure, and the final supremum logic.

claims.plain: Under a scale-critical Morrey Type-I bound, every terminal singular point carries fixed critical L3 mass in a parabolic ball at every sufficiently late time.
claims.formal: For a finite-energy Leray-Hopf solution on R3 whose first blow-up time is T-star, if the scale-critical local L2 Morrey quantity is uniformly bounded and (x-star,T-star) is singular, then fixed gamma>0 and K<infinity give ||u(t)||_L3(B_{K sqrt(T-star-t)}(x-star))>gamma for every sufficiently late t<T-star.
assumptions: The source's scale-critical Morrey Type-I bound
claims.conclusion: Critical L3 concentration is centred at the singular point at every sufficiently late time.
claims.audit: The theorem is every-late-time rather than merely limsup, but it requires the Type-I Morrey bound and does not place an independently selected carrier near the singular centre.

claims.plain: A weak-L3 Type-I singularity carries logarithmically growing strong L3 mass between its parabolic scale and every admissible larger radius.
claims.formal: For a mild R3 solution with unit viscosity, weak-L3 Type-I bound M, and a singular point (x-star,T-star), Barker-Prange Theorem A gives integral over B_R(x-star) of |u(t)| cubed at least exp(-exp(M^1025)) times log(R squared divided by M^802(T-star-t)) whenever R exceeds 2 sqrt((T-star-t)/S_BP(M)) and lies below the fixed source upper radius.
assumptions: The source's radius and time range
claims.conclusion: The local strong L3 mass has a quantitative logarithmic lower bound across the Type-I scale range.
claims.audit: The lower bound is valid at every admissible radius and late time, but by itself it neither localizes one tensor detector nor excludes weak-L3 scale towers.

claims.plain: A suitable finite-energy weak-L3 Type-I solution has only finitely many singular points at a fixed terminal time.
claims.formal: Barker-Prange Corollary 4.3 bounds the number of blow-up points at T-star by exp(exp(M^1024)) for a global suitable finite-energy unit-viscosity solution with L-infinity-in-time weak-L3 norm at most M before T-star.
assumptions: The global weak-L3 Type-I bound before the terminal time
claims.conclusion: The terminal singular set is finite with a quantitative source bound.
claims.audit: Finiteness isolates each terminal singular point from the others, but it gives no uniform lower bound for the regularity radius at nearby regular points.

claims.plain: A local-energy solution with uniformly bounded unit-ball L2 initial data has a quantitative uniform local-energy and pressure window.
claims.formal: For a unit-viscosity R3 local-energy solution whose initial datum has supremum over unit balls of the L2 norm squared at most M squared, Barker-Prange Proposition A.3 gives a time k1 min(M^-4,1), a uniform unit-ball kinetic-energy and dissipation bound K1 M squared, and a local/nonlocal pressure decomposition with quantitative spacetime bounds.
assumptions: Initial data in local L2 with vanishing unit-ball L2 mass at spatial infinity
A uniform supremum of the initial unit-ball L2 norm
claims.conclusion: Local kinetic energy, dissipation, and normalized pressure are uniformly controlled for a positive time depending only on the unit-ball initial bound.
claims.audit: The proposition supplies compactness and suitability, not regularity. For rescaled finite-energy data the decay-at-infinity hypothesis holds at each index, while weak-L3 gives the uniform unit-ball L2 ceiling.

claims.plain: A locally compact sequence of suitable solutions whose local essential suprema blow up at every radius has a singular suitable limit.
claims.formal: Albritton-Barker Proposition 2.3 states the interior persistence-of-singularities principle for suitable weak solutions: under the proposition's uniform local velocity and pressure bounds and compact convergence, divergence of the approximating local L-infinity norms on every cylinder forces the limit point to be singular.
assumptions: Uniform local suitable-solution velocity and pressure bounds from the source proposition
Approximating local essential suprema are unbounded on every cylinder
claims.conclusion: The corresponding point of the suitable weak limit is singular.
claims.audit: The proposition transfers singularity; it does not infer the required prelimit L-infinity divergence. For a moving singular point one must first translate the sequence and pass the centres to a limit.

claims.plain: A suitable solution with a locally uniform weak-L3 endpoint has a locally finite singular set at each selected time.
claims.formal: Seregin Theorem 1.2 states that if a suitable weak solution on a space-time cylinder is locally in L-infinity-in-time L3-infinity-in-space, then the singular points at the terminal time are finite in every compactly contained spatial subdomain.
claims.conclusion: Only finitely many terminal singular points lie in that subdomain.
claims.audit: The theorem gives local rather than global finiteness. Local finiteness is sufficient to forbid the inward dilation orbit of any noncentral singular point in an exactly discretely self-similar terminal profile.

claims.plain: Existence of a suitable Type-I singular point is equivalent to existence of a nontrivial mild bounded ancient solution with finite scale-invariant Type-I quantity.
claims.formal: Albritton-Barker Theorem 1.1 states that there exists a suitable weak solution with a Type-I singular point if and only if there exists a nontrivial mild bounded ancient R3 solution with finite source quantity I.
assumptions: The source definition of a suitable weak solution and Type-I singularity
The source definition of a mild bounded ancient solution and scale-invariant quantity I
claims.conclusion: The two existence statements are equivalent.
claims.audit: The forward implication produces an ancient profile from a Type-I singularity, but it does not assert that an independently obtained suitable compactification is itself mild or that the two profiles coincide.

claims.plain: Weak L3-infinity solutions defined by a heat part plus an energy correction are stable under bounded weak-star convergence of their initial data.
claims.formal: If divergence-free initial data a_k converge weak-star to a in L3-infinity and v_k are Barker-Seregin-Sverak weak L3-infinity solutions launched from a_k, then a subsequence converges distributionally to a weak L3-infinity solution launched from a; the energy-correction bounds on each finite interval depend only on the common initial weak-L3 bound and elapsed time.
assumptions: Divergence-free initial data bounded and weak-star convergent in L3-infinity
The source correction and local energy inequalities
claims.conclusion: A subsequence converges to a weak L3-infinity solution with the limiting initial trace.
claims.audit: This stability class is narrower than general local-energy suitability. A finite-energy Leray-Hopf solution restarted at a good time with datum in L3-infinity enters the class after subtracting the heat flow and verifying the correction energy inequality.

claims.plain: A mild weak-L3-controlled ancient solution whose terminal trace is sufficiently close to the critical-blow-down-vanishing Besov subspace is zero.
claims.formal: For every M there is epsilon(M)>0 such that an R3 mild ancient solution with L3-infinity norm at most M along times tending to minus infinity is identically zero if its terminal trace has dot-B-minus-one-infinity-infinity distance at most epsilon(M) from the subspace whose Navier-Stokes critical blow-downs lambda f(lambda dot) vanish distributionally.
assumptions: A backward sequence with uniformly bounded weak L3 norm
The proof-consistent critical-dilation reading of the source blow-down subspace
Small terminal distance to that subspace
claims.conclusion: The ancient solution vanishes identically.
claims.audit: Theorem 4.1 is peer reviewed, but the displayed source definition omits the critical amplitude factor that its own rescaling proof needs. Independent adversarial review validated the proof-consistent reading and found no published erratum, so it remains labelled a repair rather than a verbatim citation. Extending the proof from mildness to an independently supplied coherent weak-L3 restart structure is a repository conditional theorem, not part of this imported claim.

claims.plain: A mild ancient solution whose strong L3 norm stays bounded along one sequence of times tending to minus infinity is zero.
claims.formal: If u is a mild ancient R3 solution and t_k decreases to minus infinity with supremum over k of ||u(t_k)||_L3 finite, then u is identically zero.
assumptions: A backward sequence t_k tending to minus infinity with uniformly bounded strong L3 norm
claims.conclusion: The ancient solution vanishes identically.
claims.audit: Uniform weak L3 is not uniform strong L3, and the theorem cannot be applied to a suitable local-energy profile until mildness and one bounded strong-L3 backward sequence are proved.

claims.plain: It is open whether every smooth three-dimensional unforced initial state remains smooth globally.
claims.formal: Neither Clay alternative A nor B has an accepted proof.
assumptions: Arbitrary admissible smooth divergence-free initial data
claims.conclusion: Global smoothness remains unknown.
claims.audit: Recheck official status for future snapshots.
